name: Cleveland-Cliffs Inc.

profile.description: Cleveland-Cliffs Inc. is the largest flat-rolled steel producer in North America, headquartered in Cleveland, Ohio. Founded in 1847 originally as an iron ore mining company, Cleveland-Cliffs transformed into an integrated steelmaker through the acquisitions of AK Steel (2020), ArcelorMittal USA (2020), FPT Company (2021), and Stelco Holdings (2024). The company serves automotive OEMs, infrastructure, distributors, and manufacturers with hot-rolled, cold-rolled, coated, stainless, and electrical steel products, and also supplies iron ore pellets and direct reduced iron. CEO Lourenco Goncalves has led the company since 2014.

innovation.label: DEVELOPING
innovation.analysis: Track Record (2/3): Cleveland-Cliffs has executed one of the most significant transformations in American industrial history — pivoting from a single-product iron ore pellet miner to a fully integrated flat-rolled steel producer via four major acquisitions between 2020 and 2024 (AK Steel, ArcelorMittal USA, FPT, Stelco). This redefined the company entirely and qualifies as a transformative pivot [1][3]. However, the most recent Stelco acquisition is still being integrated and revenue has been declining since 2022 ($22.99B → $18.61B), tempering the track record score. AI Deployment (1/2): In April 2026, Cleveland-Cliffs announced a three-year partnership with Palantir to embed AI (Foundry and AIP platforms) into production planning, order entry, and operational workflows across its flat-rolled steel manufacturing footprint — a meaningful, company-specific AI deployment with quantified complexity benefits [2]. Initial pilots demonstrated significant operational benefits in managing steelmaking complexity. Market Share (1/2): Cleveland-Cliffs is the largest flat-rolled steel producer in North America and the dominant domestic automotive steel supplier, winning a major GM contract in 2025. However, it has not been actively gaining share — revenue has been declining and the attempted U.S. Steel acquisition (blocked by Biden administration) would have extended leadership further [4]. Product Pipeline (1/2): The Palantir AI integration represents a specific, named next-generation operational platform with a 3-year timeline and defined scope across production planning and order management [2]. No next-generation steel products with specific launch dates and revenue visibility disclosed. R&D Intensity (0/1): Steel is capital-intensive but not R&D-intensive; Cleveland-Cliffs' R&D spend as a percentage of revenue is below industrial sector averages, and the company focuses on operational efficiency and M&A rather than product innovation pipelines.

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (2/4): Cleveland-Cliffs' stated purpose centers on being the leading American steel producer with a commitment to union workers and domestic industrial strength. CEO Goncalves consistently articulates a vision of revitalizing American manufacturing and protecting union steelworker jobs, which is specific and distinctive for the industry [1][3]. However, this reads more as a strategic positioning and competitive identity than a transformative Just Cause — it defines what they do and for whom, but does not connect to a larger vision of a better future beyond 'American steel wins.' Community Impact (1/2): Cleveland-Cliffs has strong community ties through its steelworker union relationships (USW), support for manufacturing communities in Ohio, Michigan, and Indiana, and its role as a major employer in regions with few alternatives. CEO Goncalves earned recognition for being 'union-friendly' in an industry that typically combats unions [3]. However, no specific foundation, dollar-denominated charitable program, or community development investment was found. Employee Retention (1/2): The company has approximately 25,000 employees and Goncalves has earned the S&P Global Platts 'CEO/Chairperson of the Year' award for being 'admired and followed by employees' [3]. USW union contract stability provides a structural floor for retention in steelmaking facilities. No specific voluntary turnover or tenure data available. Insider Buying (0/2): Net insider selling is the clear signal — CEO Lourenco Goncalves sold 3,000,000 shares in February 2026 at $12.41/share ($37.2M total), and COO Clifford T. Smith sold 200,000 shares at the same time ($2.1M). While an EVP purchased 9,500 shares in February 2025, the overwhelming net is insider selling by the most senior leaders [5][6].

infrastructure.label: DEVELOPING
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored using growth stock weighting. CLF passes the liquidity floor ($12.83 × 17.5M avg vol = $225M daily). D/E of 129.3% scores 0.742 — elevated but not at the 300% floor. ROE of -18.6% scores 0.0 (negative equity return; 2x weight). Revenue YoY of -3.0% (2025: $18.61B vs 2024: $19.19B) scores 0.567. Revenue 3-year CAGR of -6.8% scores 0.0 (below the -5% floor; 2x weight). Forward P/E of 29.2 scores 0.769. Profit margin of -6.4% scores 0.0 (negative; losses driven by high raw material costs, elevated interest expense, and steel price compression in 2025). Forward PEG of 0.43 scores 1.0 (2x weight) — the street's earnings recovery expectation is priced very cheaply. Despite the low PEG reflecting analyst optimism, the current fundamental picture is challenged — negative ROE, negative profit margins, and 3-year revenue CAGR below the floor collectively constrain the score to 4.

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (8/9): Steel manufacturing is a physical, capital-intensive, energy-intensive, and safety-critical process that AI structurally cannot replace. Cleveland-Cliffs operates blast furnaces, electric arc furnaces, rolling mills, and coating lines that require physical infrastructure, metallurgical expertise, human operators, and decades of process knowledge. AI cannot produce hot-rolled coil or automotive steel — it can only optimize the humans and systems that do. No analyst has flagged AI substitution risk in integrated steelmaking, and AI is actively increasing steel demand through data center infrastructure construction (server racks, structural steel) [2][4]. AI Opportunity (0/1): While the Palantir AI partnership is a meaningful operational deployment, it is an internal efficiency tool — not a product or service from which Cleveland-Cliffs generates AI revenue. The company does not sell AI capabilities and has not disclosed quantified AI-specific revenue contributions. Score reflects AI as a cost optimizer rather than a revenue generator.

infinite_game.summary: Cleveland-Cliffs under Goncalves shows genuine existential flexibility — turning an iron ore miner into North America's leading integrated steelmaker through sheer strategic boldness. The union commitment and industrial revival mission give the company a real cause. But Goncalves' combative leadership style, aggressive financial leverage for acquisitions, and the CEO's $37M personal stock sale in early 2026 raise real questions about whether this is an infinite player or a finite one playing a very long game.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (1/3): Goncalves has consistently articulated a cause around reviving American integrated steelmaking and protecting domestic union jobs — a mission that is specific and connects to something larger than profit [1][3]. His pursuit of U.S. Steel was framed explicitly as protecting the national interest and American steelworkers. However, Sinek would note this 'cause' is primarily competitive and nationalistic — defined by what it protects (American steel, union jobs) rather than a vision of a future state that does not yet exist. The company's strategic narrative is reactive to China's steel dominance rather than generative of a new possibility. It scores as shallow-genuine rather than deeply transformative.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (1/2): Cleveland-Cliffs earns real credit for its union-friendly posture — Goncalves has maintained and expanded USW union relationships across all acquired facilities, a meaningful signal of trust with the workforce [3]. S&P Global Platts recognized him as 'admired and followed by employees.' However, Goncalves is also known for castigating analysts and short-sellers, which signals a combative external culture that can seep inward. The 2024 Stelco integration, adding thousands of Canadian workers, creates organizational complexity that strains trusting team culture.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (0/1): Goncalves is a deeply finite competitor — known for publicly attacking Nippon Steel, lobbying aggressively against the U.S. Steel acquisition by Nippon, and framing competitive battles as zero-sum wins [1][3]. His language is consistently about defeating rivals, protecting market share, and 'winning' the American steel market. Sinek would identify this as a canonical example of finite competitive mindset. No evidence of framing rivals as sources of self-improvement or acknowledging competitors' strengths as motivation for internal development.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (2/2): This is Cleveland-Cliffs' strongest Sinek dimension. The company executed one of the most dramatic strategic pivots in American industrial history — from a niche iron ore pellet miner (pre-2020) to the largest flat-rolled steel producer in North America — through four transformational acquisitions in five years [1][3]. This required abandoning the familiar mining identity, taking on substantial debt ($7.25B total debt), and betting the company on the thesis that domestic integrated steelmaking could be revived. Whether this pivot is driven by Just Cause or opportunism is debatable, but the transformation itself is textbook existential flexibility.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (2/2): Cleveland-Cliffs' M&A track record demonstrates genuine courage — acquiring AK Steel and ArcelorMittal USA simultaneously at the nadir of the COVID-19 pandemic (2020) when steel demand had collapsed, and attempting a $7.2B bid for U.S. Steel that was ultimately blocked by the Biden administration [1][3]. CEO Goncalves made the Palantir AI partnership announcement while the company was reporting losses — investing in long-term operational capability during earnings weakness. The Stelco acquisition in November 2024 (adding Canadian operations) while domestic steel prices were under pressure further demonstrates a pattern of counter-cyclical boldness.

investment_thesis: Cleveland-Cliffs is a high-risk, high-leverage turnaround story trading at a deep discount to earnings recovery potential. The Forward PEG of 0.43 implies the market is pricing in significant earnings recovery from the current loss position (-$2.31 trailing EPS) to positive forward EPS of $0.44 — a substantial improvement driven by steel price normalization, tariff tailwinds from the Trump administration's 25% steel import tariffs, and the Q1 2026 signal from CEO Goncalves that the 'order book is full' as steel prices recover [4]. The Palantir AI integration represents a genuine long-term operational differentiator — Palantir's Foundry and AIP platforms are expected to improve scheduling complexity and reduce production variability across CLF's 15+ manufacturing facilities [2]. The GM supply contract win and full automotive market integration make CLF the de facto domestic automotive steel supplier.
thesis_continued: The primary concerns are leverage, execution, and leadership signals. With $7.25B in total debt (D/E 129.3%), negative ROE of -18.6%, and negative profit margins of -6.4%, CLF has minimal financial cushion against steel price volatility or demand weakness. Revenue has declined for three consecutive years ($22.99B → $21.99B → $19.19B → $18.61B) and the 3-year CAGR of -6.8% reflects structural headwinds, not just cyclicality [8]. Most concerning: CEO Goncalves sold 3,000,000 shares ($37.2M) in February 2026, and COO Smith sold an additional 200,000 shares the same week — the most senior insiders substantially reducing exposure during the supposed recovery [5][6]. The Stelco integration complexity, ongoing CRE cost pressures, and history of aggressive acquisition leverage create a fragile capital structure that depends on steel price recovery materializing on schedule.
key_catalysts: Steel price recovery driven by U.S. tariff policy (25% Section 232 tariffs on steel imports) tightening domestic supply and expanding CLF's automotive contract margins above current loss levels
Palantir AI platform deployment across production planning and order entry generating measurable cost savings and capacity utilization improvements by late 2026
GM and automotive OEM contract volume expansion as EV platform production (steel-intensive battery enclosures and structural components) ramps through 2026-2027
key_risks: CEO Lourenco Goncalves sold 3,000,000 shares ($37.2M) in February 2026 while the company was reporting losses — the most significant insider selling signal against a recovery thesis

3-year revenue CAGR of -6.8% and consecutive years of declining revenue from $23B to $18.6B reflect structural headwinds from cheap imported steel and automotive industry electrification uncertainty that tariffs alone may not reverse

Balance sheet fragility: $7.25B debt with negative profit margins and negative ROE creates limited margin of safety if steel prices fail to recover sufficiently in 2026, risking covenant pressure on the debt load
